text: In comparison to dorsal rectopexy, be it a posterior sutured rectopexy or a dorsal mesh rectopexy, ventral rectopexy has been found to give better functional outcomes in terms of constipation scores as well as in terms of quality of life.
The reason for this improved functional outcome is that in the ventral rectopexy, the rectal ampulla is not mobilized and the lateral ligaments are not divided as we do in the dorsal rectopexy.
This mobilization of the ampulla and division of the lateral ligaments has been found to increase GI transit time and worsen constipation.
In ventral rectopexy, the lateral and posterior mobilization of the rectum is avoided. Thereby, the chance of autonomic nerve injury with subsequent urinary and sexual dysfunction is minimal.

The rectal prolapse always starts with the internal interception. The interception always starts in the anterior wall of the rectum. The ventral rectopexy addresses the lead point of the interception better.

Also, most patients with rectal prolapse have a deep cul-de-sac into which they can have an enterocele containing the small bowel or the sigmoid. This is also treated during ventral rectopexy unlike in dorsal.
Likewise, the ventral rectopexy can be used to correct simultaneous vaginal prolapse as well as pelvic floor descent.
The surgeon positions the laparoscopic ports in standard locations for rectal surgery, preparing for an initial visual assessment of the pelvic region.
The video shows a deep cul-de-sac in the pelvic region, with visibly thickened peritoneum due to recurrent rectal prolapse.
The surgeon begins laparoscopic rectal mobilization by opening the lateral peritoneal fold of the rectum and entering the DME plane.
The laparoscopic view reveals the inferior mesenteric vessels positioned anteriorly, while posteriorly, the hypogastric nerves are visible, covered by loose areolar tissue.
During dorsal mobilization of the rectum, the surgeon dissects on the left side, identifying the left ureter and gonadal vessels amidst the surrounding tissue.
In the ventral rectopexy procedure, the surgeon performs a limited dissection in the retrorectal space, exposing the sacral promontory.
The surgeon extends the peritoneal incision along the lateral peritoneal fold of the rectum in a cranio-caudal direction, reaching the anterior rectum at the cul-de-sac.
The surgeon makes an incision solely on the peritoneum, avoiding medial dissection and preserving the lateral ligaments, contrasting with dorsal rectum mobilization techniques.

The surgeon performs anterior dissection for ventral rectopexy, advancing along the surface of the anterior rectal wall. In women, the dissection occurs posterior to the rectovaginal septum, while in men, it is posterior to the dental villus fascia.

The surgeon exposes the anterior rectal wall to prepare for mesh fixation onto the rectal musculature, ensuring close proximity to the rectal wall to avoid injury to the anterior pelvic plexus of nerves.
The surgeon meticulously dissects the anterior cul-de-sac, carefully avoiding injury to the thickened and scarred rectal wall due to recurrent prolapse.
The surgeon dissects the anterior rectal wall down to the level of the pelvic floor muscles and the perineal body, exposing the muscles of the pelvic diaphragm laterally.
The surgeon exposes the anterior rectal wall without performing lateral dissection, creating a tunnel in front of the anterior rectal wall for mesh placement.
The surgeon identifies the lower limit of the anterior rectal dissection, approximately three to four centimeters from the anal verge, using a per-rectal examination during the mobilization process.

In patients with significant pelvic floor descent, the surgeon exposes the pelvic floor musculature to the diaphragm. The mesh is cut into a T-shape, with its lateral edges fixed to the pelvic floor diaphragm. If there is a vaginal vault prolapse, the mesh is extended and sutured to the vaginal vault to address both vaginal and rectal prolapse.

After completing the mobilization, the surgeon prepares to place a 15x5 cm proline mesh over the anterior rectal wall, measuring the distance from the lowermost point of fixation to the sacral promontory for accurate placement.
The surgeon uses duo Ethibond sutures to fix the lower end of the mesh to the anterior rectal musculature at the lowermost point of mobilization, approximately four centimeters from the anal verge.

The surgeon takes tangential bites of the rectal musculature, ensuring not to enter the rectal lumen, while securing the mesh to the anterior rectal wall. The mesh is fixed at the lowermost point of mobilization, and the surgeon proceeds proximally, taking bites one centimeter apart along either end of the mesh.
The surgeon secures the lower end of the mesh to the anterior rectal wall by placing two to three sets of sutures along either edge of the mesh, spaced one centimeter apart.

The surgeon places the lowermost sutures between the mesh and the anterior rectal wall deep inside the tunnel, navigating the limited space with precision. The robotic system's enhanced maneuverability aids in this intricate task compared to traditional laparoscopic techniques.

The surgeon continues laparoscopic suturing of the mesh to the anterior rectal wall musculature, progressing proximally with increased ease. The lower end of the mesh is secured with four to six sutures.
The surgeon places additional sutures higher up on the lateral rectal wall and the mesorectum to evenly distribute tension across the mesh.

The surgeon fixes the upper end of the mesh to the sacral promontory using permanent metal tackers, ensuring the mesh is taut but not overly tight to avoid altering the anorectal angle. The pelvic fascia overlying the sacral promontory is visible as the mesh is secured.
The surgeon uses transfacial sutures to secure the mesh and the pelvic fascia overlying the sacral promontory, complementing the tacker fixation technique similar to laparoscopic hernia repair.
The surgeon performs tachyfixation, ensuring the mesh remains within the gutter formed by the lateral peritoneal incision, while carefully avoiding injury to the inframucentric artery and iliac vein.

The surgeon uses a V-lock suture to close the peritoneum over the mesh, reconstituting the cul-de-sac and obliterating the deep cul-de-sac associated with rectal prolapse. This closure prevents mesh exposure to the peritoneal cavity and avoids bubble adhesions.

To conclude, ventral rectopexy offers better functional outcomes compared to dorsal rectopexy. It also addresses the other problems of the anterior compartment such as walled prolapse, pelvic floor descent and enterocele. In addition, it avoids autonomic nerve injury with urinary and sexual dysfunction.
